lesson_title: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text:
To define a struct: {{c1::struct}} Employee {{c2::{
  int id;
  double wage;
};}}}
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.front: What are the variables inside a struct called?
cards.back:
Data members or member variables.
Each object gets its own copy.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text: To access struct member: employee{{c1::.}}id (using {{c2::member selection operator}})
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: Accesses the wage member of joe struct object.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.front: What is the naming convention for struct types?
cards.back:
Start with capital letter, use PascalCase:
Employee, DateOfBirth, InventoryItem
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.question: What values do joe's members have?
cards.code:
struct Employee { int id; double wage; };
Employee joe;
cards.answer: Indeterminate (garbage values) - members are not initialized.
cards.explanation: Uninitialized struct members with fundamental types contain garbage.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.front: What are the benefits of using structs over separate variables?
cards.back:
• Group related data together
  • Pass multiple values as one parameter
  • Return multiple values from functions
  • Create arrays of related objects
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text: Structs in C++ can contain both {{c1::data members}} and {{c2::member functions}}.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.front: Where should the struct type name be placed?
cards.back:
After the closing brace:
struct Employee { /* ... */ };
Not C-style: struct { } Employee;
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.explanation:
Use structs when:
  • Grouping related data that represents a single concept
  • Data is frequently passed/returned together
  • You want arrays of complex objects
  • All members should be public (no encapsulation needed)
Use class when you need encapsulation/invariants.
examples: struct Point { int x; int y; };
struct Employee { int id; std::string name; };
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.front: Does each struct object get its own copy of the members?
cards.back: Yes. Each object has its own separate copy of all data members.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text: Struct type names conventionally use {{c1::PascalCase}} starting with a {{c2::capital letter}}.
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text: To define a struct member: struct Employee { {{c1::int}} id; {{c2::double}} wage; };
cards.source: 13.7 — Introduction to structs, members, and member selection

cards.text: Struct type names conventionally use {{c1::PascalCase}} starting with a {{c2::capital letter}}.
